  • ods — Reading ODS files »

The ODS class¶

Please check the ODS - reading and filtering ODS files tutorial for examples of how to use ODS and how to write filtering expressions.

note:In order to use Python’s expression parsing capabilities, the ODS and the ODSVariable classes make use of operator overloading. However, since the operators and, or and not cannot be redefined, the bitwise operators &, | and ~ are used. This is not semantically wrong since we handle arrays of booleans. a & b where a and b are arrays of booleans with the same shape, returns an array of booleans with the same shape.
warning:the operator precedence in Python is such that bitwise operators have higher precedence than comparison operators. The expression a < 2 & c > 3 is parsed executing 2 & c first. You should therefore be careful to uses brackets (parenthesis) to modify the precedence: (a < 2) & (c > 3). This is slightly inconvenient, but it is at least never ambiguous.
class gmaopy.ods.ods.ODS(filename, masking=None)¶

The ODS class provides support reading ODS files and selecting observations according to different criteria.

__init__(filename, masking=None)¶
Parameters:
  • filename (string) – ods file to be read
  • masking (callable) – a callable which is given a chance to carry out extra data masking

Opens the specified file.

compile(expression, used_observations=True, **kargs)¶
Parameters:
  • expression (string) – a truth expression containing floating point literals and making reference to variable names in the netcdf file (ods file).
  • used_observations (bool) – by default set to True, only observations used in the analysis are processed. If set to False, all observations are processed, in that case the variable qcexcl can be part of the expression.

Compiles a text based expression by parsing it and executing it. The value returned is an object instance of class ODSVariable containing a numpy array of boolean. It has the same shape as the variables which are part of the expression and can be used to extract data using the mask.

extract(name, filter=None)¶
Parameters:
  • name (string) – the name of the variable to extract.
  • filter (numpy array of booleans or a :class: ODSVariable) – a filter (or mask) as returned by the method compile.

Returns a numpy array for the specified variable. If not filter is provided, the whole variable is returned, otherwise an array of the size corresponding to the number of True values in the filter is returned with values taken from the specified variable.

Synonym :read
sampleLevels(variable_name='lev', levels=None, bins=None)¶
Parameters:
  • variableName (string) – name of the variable in the ODS file (by default it is ‘lev’)
  • levels (float or list of floats) – list of levels to consider, all if not specified
  • bins (float or list of floats) – list of bins to use for binning

This method loads the variable containing all the observations levels (in variableName) and matches them to the list of observations provided in argument levelList. The closest match is stored in an array to allow further selection of observations. The method returns an instance of class LevelFilter. The filter returned by this method is valid for one single ODS file, one filter if specified and a list of levels. If any of these 3 criteria changes, the method sampleLevels should be called again (see method validateLevelFilter).

Warning :matches are found by choosing the closest level specified in levelList to the level contained in the ‘lev’ variable independently of their “distance”. If levelList contains only a few levels, some matches may not make sense.
sampleLinearLevels(variable_name='lev', levels=None, bins=None)¶
Parameters:
  • variableName (string) – name of the variable in the ODS file (by default it is ‘lev’)
  • levels (float or list of floats) – list of levels to consider, all if not specified
  • bins (float or list of floats) – list of bins to use for binning

This method is identical to sampleLevels() except that unlike pressure levels, the levels are linear, this is useful for instrument channels and ocean depth.

validateObsFilter(filter)¶
Parameters:filter (ObsFilter) – a filter returned by the method compile

This method checks that the filter provided was computed by that ODS instance. A ValueError exception is raised if it is not the case.

validateLevelFilter(levels, levelfilter)¶
Parameters:
  • levels (float or list of floats) – levels which should have been matched
  • levelfilter (LevelFilter) – instance returned by the method sampleLevels

This method makes sure that the filter levelfilter was computed for the same file and the same list of levels. If there is a mismatch, a ValueError exception is raised. You can use this method for debugging purposes to make sure that you are associating the right filters with the right data.
